The main claim is: Originally, Kolmogorov complexity measured the complexity of bit strings.
In other words, the successor program has Kolmogorov complexity less than or equal to that of its predecessor, but so what?
The main claim is: Yes, it is possible for kinetic friction to be higher than static friction.
So, if I had two very rough surfaces, then the kinetic friction between them would exceed their static friction?
The main claim is: Cognitive biases are not biases; they are engines of cognition, each exploiting regularities of the universe.
These cognitive biases initially arose through the directed search process of evolution.

I am not actually convinced that this much smarter AI can solve problems that humans will never solve?
The main claim is: People wildly underestimate how much influence individuals can have in policy.
Like 95% of the immediate problems of AI policy are just "who has power under capitalism", and you literally can't do anything about it.
